But that is the answer for a HEADING of 200. The question asks for a TRACK of 200. You now have to lay off for drift, which will give you a heading of 217 at the top, balanced by 17 degrees of port drift, giving a track of 200. 2 c 3 b We recommend that you use the CRP-5 for true altitude problems, especially if the question gives you the actual SAT instead of ISA deviation. But if you want to use the formula, the ISA deviation is +30 degrees: True Alt = 5000 + (+30 x 4/1000 x 5000) = 5600 feet which is pretty close to answer (b). 4 d You would expect the greatest drift when the wind direction makes the greatest angle with from the track or reciprocal direction (the JAA use the word ‘course’ to mean ‘desired track’). The reciprocal track of 040° is 220° and answer (d) is the furthest from 220°. 5 c 6 a You should note that these longitudes are meridian and anti-meridian. This is the over- the-pole case. 7 a If your compass heading is 090 C, you can fill in the following table: T V M D C 100 12E 088 2W 090  So your true heading is 100. VOR radials are always magnetic, so you are flying a true track of 082. Your TAS is 160. If you have gone 14 nm in 6 mins, your G/S is 140. Now use your CRP-5 as in Chapter 6. 8 b This question is solved by scale drawing. Use the scale to draw a line 480 nm along the 110°E meridian from the North Pole, then plot a line 300 nm in a direction of 154°Grid. 9 b 10 c This is a departure question. A very similar example is given on in Chapter 15 11 c 12 a 13 a 14 a 15 b

subject: NAV
text: Chapter 38 Revision Questions 16 a 17 c With a Direct Reading Compass, the compass needle or magnetic assembly (for instance, the magnets in the centre of the vertical card) have to physically take up the alignment of north, which involves overcoming friction. With a Slaved Gyro Compass, the 3 legs of the flux valve pick up different amounts of magnetic flux on different headings. No friction needs to be overcome when the aircraft alters heading and so the system is more sensitive. 18 a 19 b 20 c 21 d 22 c This question is intended to check understanding of the difference between INS and IRS (as fitted to FMS). In old-fashioned INS it was possible to put in an incorrect initial latitude and longitude and the system would accept the information without checks. If the longitude was in error, the system would never know and, apart from the fact that it was showing the incorrect position, it would align and operate normally. However, if latitude was in error it would adversely affect the alignment, because the gyrocompassing process uses sine and cosine latitude as part of the alignment calculation. If the latitude was more than about, say, ten degrees out, the system would probably not align at all. Therefore you can say that the INS accepts incorrect longitude but rejects latitude error. IRS/FMS is more modern technology and has greater computing power and a non- volatile memory, It therefore remembers where it was shut down after the last flight. When you input your present position on at start-up on the next flight, if it is more than a certain distance (about 5 km, to allow for moving the aircraft to another part of the airport), the software rejects the new position. We therefore say that IRS/FMS rejects incorrect latitude or longitude. 23 b 24 c A process known as Kalman filtering compares the positions generated by each IRS with a series of DME/DME cross-cut fixes. The history of the pattern of IRS errors is built up over time and this data is used to build a mathematical model of IRS errors, which is then used to calculate the statistically most probable position This is not the same thing as the average of the DME/DME and IRS positions. . 25 b Answers: Waypoint names – eg, BLUFA navaid identifiers – eg, CPT runway numbers – eg, 27L airport ICAO identifiers – eg, EGTK. Many systems do not use waypoint numbers. Navaid frequencies are not unique and navaid positions would use more than 5 alpha-numerics. ¬ 26 d A very similar question is in Chapter 14

subject: NAV
text: Revision Questions Chapter 38 27 b FMS is invariably associated with IRS rather than INS. One of the changes which has taken place over the last 20-30 years of technological development is a vast increase in computer memory store. Unlike the older INS, IRS has a data base which can look up a value of variation for a given latitude and longitude position. The IRS is aligned to True North, then calculates Magnetic North from the data base variation. 28 a The given answer is a direct quote from a recommended CAA publication, so we are certain that it is the required option. However, it is a questionable statement. Manual input from the pilots is required during initialisation and we would describe the procedure as semi-automatic. Nevertheless, (a) is the ‘correct’ answer. 29 c FMS Chapter. See under Illuminated Annunciators – MSG 30 d 31 b After initial alignment and insertion of initial position, the IRS position is not further updated. It gets passed to the Kalman filter in the FMC where it is error modelled against the DME/DME fixes. The FMC position is updated in flight but the IRS position is not. 32 c Answer (d) is just noise. Of the other options, if you’ve got only 2 systems, not externally monitored against DME/DME or GPS, how can you tell which one is correct? Each is as likely to be correct as the other. Indeed, they might both be a bit wrong. 33 d The B737-400 uses DME/DME as the primary source to be Kalman-filtered against the IRS. However, if the DME/DME angle of cut is poor, it can revert to VOR/DME if the DME range is less than 30 nm. If the options given are ‘DME/DME’ or ‘VOR/DME’, choose ‘DME/DME’ because ‘VOR/DME’ on its own is wrong. But if ‘VOR/DME and DME/DME’ is an option, that is the one to go for. 34 c If you have 3 different IRSs, each will have slightly different errors (for instance, slightly different initial alignments, slightly different laser gyro wander rates, etc) which will result in slightly different computations of IRS position. 35 c Gyro-compassing is the final stage of alignment, after caging and levelling. During alignment, it is essential that the aircraft is not moved. The principle of both levelling and gyro-compassing depends on the accelerometers detecting the direction of gravity (ie, vertically downwards) very accurately. If there is any horizontal acceleration it will be detected along with the acceleration due to gravity and the direction sensed will be the resultant of the 2 accelerations, which will not be vertical. 36 d With IRS, the initial present position is usually entered via the FMS, so there is no need for the STBY position used in the INS. 37 c 74 nm @ the planned G/S of 115 knots = 38.6 mins 250 nm @ the planned G/S of 115 knots = 130.4 mins After 74 nm, the actual elapsed time = 40.1 mins Therefore the flight time for 250 nm at the revised G/S = 130.4 x 40.1 / 38/6 This comes to 135 mins. 0900UTC + 135 mins = 1115UTC

subject: NAV
text: Chapter 38 Revision Questions 38 b There are 2 separate effects here, both dependent on time. Firstly, the heading error will increase with time, ie, if the azimuth gyro drift rate is 0.1 degrees/hour, then the heading will be in error by 0.1 degrees after the first hour, 0.2 degrees in error after the second hour, etc. That is the heading error. However, in addition, the cross-track position error increases with time. If there is a steady heading error of, say, one degree, then using the one in sixty rule, after 60 nm along track, the aircraft will be one nm across track, but after 120 nm along track, the aircraft will be 2 nm across track, and so on. So the cross-track error increases with time for a fixed heading error AND, with an azimuth drift rate, the heading error increases with time. These 2 effects multiply together, so the cross-track position error increases proportionally to t². 39 d The formula is X = DH / (O + H). 40 c 41 c ETA B and ETA C are not relevant. You fly 30 nm in 17 mins. How long will it take you to fly the remaining 20 nm at the same groundspeed? 42 a 43 b This is a departure problem. Convert 165 km to nm, then use the departure formula to convert the nm to change of longitude. 44 a Convert 685 km to nm, which comes to 370 nm. If you track 180°T you are flying southwards along a meridian of longitude, ie, a great circle, so one nm equals one minute of latitude. 370 nm is therefore a change of latitude of 6° 10’ S. 45 b 46 d Plotting question. Solve by measurement on the chart. 47 b This is a PNR problem. T = EH/(O+H). 48 a 49 d Find the present TAS, apply the wind and get the present G/S (230 knots) 150 nm @ 230 k G/S is 39 mins. You need to arrive 5 minutes later, so your new time to go is 44 mins. The new required G/S will be 150 nm in 44 mins, which is 205 k G/S The wind should not change, so the new required TAS will be 240 Then either use FL140/-5°C in the airspeed window to convert the TAS to 190 k CAS, or put the old TAS (264) against the old IAS (210), then against 240 TAS, you will see 190. 210 to 190 is a 20 knot reduction in IAS (strictly CAS, but the PEC should not change much in 20 knots). Answer (d). 50 b This is a PNR problem. T = EH/(O+H). 51 c This is a CP problem. X = DH/(O+H).

subject: NAV
text: Revision Questions Chapter 38 52 c ETA Y is irrelevant. If it takes you 30 minutes to cover 30 nm, it will take you a further 20 minutes to cover the remaining 20 nm at the same groundspeed. 53 b The QNH is 988 hPa. The datum for Pressure Altitude is 1013 hPa, which is 25 hPa greater. Using 27 feet to one hPa, this equates to a distance of 675 feet. A greater static pressure occurs at a lower pressure level. Therefore the 1013 hPa pressure level is below sea level by 675 feet. The airport is 1000 feet above sea level, which means that it is 1675 feet above the 1013 hPa pressure level. 54 d The aircraft flies 2950 nm N, which takes it to 4500N 17822W. Now convert 314 km to nm and then use the departure formula as in Q43. 55 d The true bearing from you TO the headland is 050°T. However, you are going to have to plot FROM the headland to the aircraft (the headland is on your map – the aircraft’s position isn’t!) The aircraft’s heading has been measured relative to the direction of True North at the aircraft’s position. If there is significant chart convergence between the aircraft’s position and the headland’s position, the direction of True North will be different at the headland. We must therefore plot the reciprocal bearing using the aircraft’s meridian paralleled through the headland’s position. 56 c 57 b Plotting question. Solve by measurement on the chart. 58 a Plotting question. Solve by measurement on the chart. 59 a 60 d Multiply by 15° per hour. The easiest way of dealing with minutes and seconds is by using the DMS function of a scientific calculator. See page 27 – 2 for details. 61 c The combination of the inclination of the ecliptic to the equator and the orbit of the Earth round the Sun produces a constantly changing declination. The length of the hours of daylight at a given latitude varies with the declination of the Sun. 62 b On a Lambert chart, the Parallel of Origin is midway between the 2 Standard Parallels. The ‘constant of the cone’ is simply another term for the sine of the parallel of origin. 63 c See Example 1 in Chapter 16, which is the same type of question. 64 a 65 b The scale on a Lambert chart alters as latitude changes but, for a fixed latitude (ie, along a parallel of latitude), it remains constant. 66 d 67 d The Jeppesen conventions differ slightly from the ICAO ones. The key is given in the introduction to the Jeppesen Student Pilots’ Manual. 68 a

subject: NAV
text: Chapter 38 Revision Questions 69 b Apply 20°E variation to 345°M to get 005°T. 30° left of this is a True bearing of 335°T from you TO the headland. However, you are going to have to plot FROM the headland to the aircraft (the headland is on your map – the aircraft’s position isn’t!) You therefore plot the reciprocal of 155°T. 70 c As per the reply to Q3. 71 c 72 b Prior to going to NAV mode, the INS/IRS will be in ALIGN mode. Gyro-compassing is the final stage of alignment, after caging and levelling. During alignment, it is essential that the aircraft is not moved. The principle of both levelling and gyro-compassing depends on the accelerometers detecting the direction of gravity (ie, vertically downwards) very accurately. If there is any horizontal acceleration it will be detected along with the acceleration due to gravity and the direction sensed will be the resultant of the 2 accelerations, which will not be vertical. Therefore the INS/IRS must be selected to NAV mode before the aircraft is moved or the alignment will be destroyed. 73 b 74 d 75 a A 12% glideslope means that for every 100 knots you go forward horizontally, you go down 12 knots vertically. 12% of your forward speed of 540 is 64.8 knots vertically. Convert this to feet per min and you will see that option (a) is the nearest 76 d 77 a 65 nm @ 240 knots will take 16.25 minutes. I need to lose 23000 feet, also in 16.25 minutes. The required ROD is 23000/16.25 = 1415 feet per minute. 78 c The output of the first-stage N/S integrator is velocity N/S. N/S is the direction of the local meridian. 79 b The Jeppesen conventions differ slightly from the ICAO ones. The key is given in the introduction to the Jeppesen Student Pilots’ Manual. 80 c 81 b 82 b The lowest latitude at which there is at least one day a year without a sunset (MidSummer Day) and one day a year without a sunrise (MidWinter Day) is the Artic Circle (which is 66½°N). Therefore the sun will rise and set every day at 62N and 66N. The higher of these two is 66N. 83 a (Pressure alt + (ISA dev x 120) gives 27560; CRP5 gives 27000) 84 a This is a PNR problem. T = EH/(O+H).

subject: NAV
text: Revision Questions Chapter 38 85 c Look up the LMT of sunrise at 49N on the 6th December. There is 3 minutes change between the 4th Dec and the 7th Dec. So, interpolating, the times at 50N and 45N are 0742 and 0723 respectively. That is a difference of 19 minutes. One-fifth of that is about 4 minutes, so the LMT of sunrise at 49N on the 6th December is 0738. Now set it out in a table: Day Hour Minute Time LMT sunrise at Vancouver 6 Dec 07 38 LMT Arc/time 123° 30`W (long west, UTC best) - +08 14 - UTC 6 Dec 15 52 UTC To calculate arc/time, enter the degrees and minutes in DMS format into your calculator and divide by 15. 86 b 87 d This is just the 1 in 60 rule. A 3-degree glide path gives you 300 feet per nautical mile. 25 nm @ 300 feet per nm is 7500 feet. That would be for a touch-down at zero feet at the runway threshold. However, height over the threshold is 50 feet, which has to be added. 88 c As always, use a table: Day Hour Minute Time Standard Time at Queensland Today 06 00 ST STD (long east, UTC least) - -10 00 - UTC Yesterday 20 00 UTC STD (long west, UTC best) - -10 00 - Standard Time at Hawaii Yesterday 1000 00 ST 89 c 90 d 91 c 92 a 93 c The first leg takes it down to the Equator at 170 W, the second leg takes it to the Equator at 143W, the third leg takes it to 27N 143W and you then work out the change of longitude of 1620 nm due west departure at 27N, which is 30°18’ W, ie 17318W. However, you don’t need to do the full calculation. You should realise that a departure of 3000 km will give a greater change of longitude at 27N than it will at the Equator. Therefore, the answer must be at 27N, but WEST of the 170W meridian. There is only one option which fits. 94 b Plotting question. Solve by measurement on the chart. 95 d You have 120 nm horizontal distance to travel at 288 knots. This will take 25 minutes. You need to lose 24000 feet in 25 minutes, which is 960 feet per minute. 96 c

subject: NAV
text: Chapter 38 Revision Questions 97 a You need to lose 6500 feet at a rate of descent of 800 feet per minute. This will take 8.125 minutes. Flying for this time at a groundspeed of 156 knots gives a distance of 21.1 nm. However, this is 6 nm before the DME, so the total distance to start the descent is 27.1 nm. 98 d ATT mode on the IRS is the same as ATT REF on the INS. If you select ATT REF (on a typical IN) or ATT on the B737-400 IRS, either after switching OFF, or directly from NAV (following a computing, but not gyro, failure), the gyros now work as a combination of a superior form of DGI and ADI. If you have a period of steady unaccelerated flight, the gyros will erect to the vertical by sensing gravity from the accelerometers and the heading can then be set by the pilot to be the same as the heading from some other source (such as a slaved gyro compass). Attitude information will stay good for the rest of flight, but the azimuth information is not corrected for Earth rate or Transport Wander, so whilst it will have the very low real drift rate associated with IN-quality gyros (in the order or 1/100 degree per hour), it still needs to be corrected every 20 minutes or so from another source because of the ER and TW drifts. However, all nav computing facilities (readouts of latitude and longitude, drift, track, waypoint steering, distance to go, etc) are no longer available. 99 d See the reply to Q22. 100 c 101 d This is a 9-degree change of latitude. You could work this out as 540 nm and then convert it to km, but there is a quicker way. 90 degrees of change of latitude is 10,000 km (see page 2 – 11), so a 9 degree change of latitude will be 1000 km. 102 c Alignment time is faster than the 17 minutes or so for an INS. There is no requirement to spin up the gyro (which takes about 3 minutes) because the laser will fire up immediately. Of the remaining 14 mins for the older technology of the INS, this time has been reduced to about 10 minutes for the IRS. 103 d The thing to realise is that on a Mercator chart, meridians are drawn as parallel lines.  Therefore if the 180E and 180W meridian are 133 cm apart at 53N, they will also be 133 cm apart at 30S. So now find the departure from 180E to 180W at 30S. Dep = ch long x cos 30 = 360 x 60 x cos 30 = 18706 nm You now have a simple scale problem. The Chart length is 133 cm, the Earth Distance is 18706. This gives you a scale of approximately 1:26 million. The nearest answer is (d). This seems a long way off, but we believe that these are the actual figures used in a JAA question. 104 d The others can be used with some equipments, but not all. 105 a

subject: NAV
text: Revision Questions Chapter 38 106 b Find the True heading in the normal way, then apply variation and deviation. First, check that you have balanced the drift. When deviation is given as East or West then you use the DEVIATION EAST COMPASS LEAST RULE. However, deviation (not variation, only deviation) is sometimes quoted as plus or minus-. In this case the rule is that deviation is what you apply to the compass to get magnetic (not the other way round). So EAST deviation is plus and WEST deviation is minus. 107 d 108 b The INS calculates heading, track and groundspeed itself, but not TAS. It needs an input of TAS from an ADC in order to calculate wind. (It does not calculate drift from the W/V or TAS, so answer c is wrong. Drift is calculated by comparing INS heading and INS track). 109 b 110 c 3 x 444 is 1332 km. Divide by 1.852 to get 719.2 nm. This has obviously been approximated to 720 to give 12° change of latitude, which takes us to latitude 02°S. 111 d The average Great Circle track is the Rhumb Line track. 112 c You should remember that the distance from the Equator to a Pole is 5400 nm (90° change of latitude x 60 nm per degree). So the Earth’s circumference is 4 x 5400, which is 21,600 nm. Alternatively, multiply 360° (a meridian plus the associated anti-meridian) by 60. 113 b 114 a The Rhumb Line track from 70S to 70S is along a parallel of latitude, so it will be 090T. However, the Great Circle track will ‘cut the corner’ and take the shortest route. This will be to the South of 090T. Draw it out on a Lambert’s style projection, or try it on a globe. The conversion angle will be 42 degrees, so this makes the initial Great Circle track 132T.

subject: NAV
text: Chapter 38 Revision Questions 115 b You can align with NAV selected on the MSU. If you go straight from STANDBY to NAV, it won’t go into NAV mode until it has finished aligning. B is correct. “What is ATT mode?” - If an INS or IRS is switched OFF in flight, either inadvertently or in response to a checklist action, then the alignment is lost. If it subsequently becomes safe to switch it back on, you cannot get a full gyro-compassing in flight because the lateral accelerometer of the East gyro will not be able to distinguish between Earth rate and accelerations caused by movement of the aircraft. This is why the aircraft has to be stationary during ground alignment. However, if you select ATT REF (on a typical IN) or ATT on the B737-400 IRS, either after switching OFF, or directly from NAV (following a computing, but not gyro, failure), the gyros now work as a combination of a superior form of DGI and ADI. If you have a period of steady unaccelerated flight, the gyros will erect to the vertical by sensing gravity from the accelerometers and the heading can then be set by the pilot to be the same as the heading from some other source (such as a slaved gyro compass). Attitude information will stay good for the rest of flight, but the azimuth information is not corrected for Earth rate or Transport Wander, so whilst it will have the very low real drift rate associated with IN-quality gyros (in the order or 1/100 degree per hour), it still needs to be corrected every 20 minutes or so from another source because of the ER and TW drifts. However, all nav computing facilities (readouts of latitude and longitude, drift, track, waypoint steering, distance to go, etc) are no longer available. 116 d 117 b 118 a 119 c 6°S is a greater value of latitude than 4°N. Therefore, for a given departure, you get a greater change of longitude eastwards. When you come back to 4°N again, 600 nm does not take you so far westwards. So you will finish up east of where you started. 120 b 121 a The gyro used for gyrocompassing is called the East gyro. The north and the east accelerometers are horizontal. All IRS and some INS have a vertical accelerometer as well, but this is not used in gyrocompassing. 122 b 123 b 124 c 125 a See my comments on Q57. 126 d 127 a CRP-5 128 b

subject: NAV
text: Revision Questions Chapter 38 129 a The primary source of information in INS/IRS is acceleration, which is measured by entirely self-contained equipment. Accelerometers do not even use gravity – they are used in space navigation. 130 a 131 c Desired track is the computed track between 2 waypoints. It is not necessarily the track being flown by the aircraft at the time, which is shown when you select TK/GS on the CDU. 132 a Either use the CRP-5 or remember that there are 5 Imperial gallons to 6 US galls and the Imp Gall to litres conversion is 4.55. Then multiply by .78 to get the weight in kg, ie: 380 x 5/6 x 4.55 x .78 = 1123.85 It’s probably easier with the CRP-5. 133 c Distance still remaining = 475 – 190 = 285 nm. Time to go = 1130 - 1040 = 50 mins 134 c Plotting question. Solve by measurement on the chart. An explanation of the point about ‘both DME distances decreasing’ is given in the Plotting chapter. 135 d The Jeppesen conventions differ slightly from the ICAO ones. The key is given in the introduction to the Jeppesen Student Pilots’ Manual. 136 a Plotting question. Solve by measurement on the chart. 137 b Plotting question. Solve by measurement on the chart. 138 d Plotting question. Solve by measurement on the chart. 139 c This is not really covered in the notes. The navigation data base of the FMS contains established waypoints (ATC reporting points, VORs, DMEs, etc). However, you may be asked to steer to some place which is not a published waypoint. In that case, the pilot has to ‘create’ a waypoint, ie, input a position into the FMS. There are various formats in which this can be done: a) Latitude and longitude b) Place bearing/Distance (ie, SAM 180035 means 35 nm on the 180 radial from Southampton VOR/DME) c) Place bearing/Place bearing (ie, SAM 135 / DVR 242 means where the 135 radial from Southampton meets the 242 radial from Dover) d) Along track distance (ie, 15 nm along the centreline of the track between SAM and DVR). However, you can’t create a “Place” - because it’s already in the data base. And you can’t input with 2 place distances because it gives 2 intersections, so the FMS doesn’t know which one to take.

subject: NAV
text: Chapter 38 Revision Questions 140 b The mean great circle is the same as the rhumb line track. The question is asking which pairs of latitudes will give the greatest difference between great circle and rhumb line track, ie, which will give the greatest conversion angle? The ca formula is 1/2 ch long sin mean lat. If you keep the 1/2 ch long constant for all cases, then you are asking which pairs of latitudes will give you the greatest sine mean lat. 141 b The longitudes are the same so, from the given lats and longs, the True bearing of the aircraft from the VOR is 000/360. For VOR, you take the variation at the VOR, because the bearing is measured at the beacon. (For an ADF bearing, it would be the variation at the aircraft, because that is where the variation is measured). 142 a The Air Vector is 5 times as long as the Wind Vector, so the direction will be nearer 180 than 179. Try drawing the Triangle of Velocities out as a diagram. 143 b 144 a 145 b 146 b 147 a The combination of the inclination of the ecliptic to the equator and the orbit of the Earth round the Sun produces a constantly changing declination. The predominant cause of the Seasons is the inclination (tilt) of the Earth. 148 c Radials radiate. That’s what the word means. In other words they are, by definition, the magnetic bearing FROM the VOR. On the VOR in the aircraft you have a needle with 2 ends. The sharp end points to the VOR and is the QDM, or mag track to the VOR and the other end points to the QDR, or radial, which is the mag bearing from the VOR. 149 d Plotting question. Solve by measurement on the chart. 150 b Plotting question. Solve by measurement on the chart. 151 a Plotting question. Solve by measurement on the chart. 152 d Plotting question. Solve by measurement on the chart. 153 a 154 c See the answer to Q98. Option (c) is the best of the available answers, although, in fact, heading is also available, albeit in a limited form. 155 b

subject: NAV
text: Revision Questions Chapter 38 156 c As the air becomes less dense, the aircraft has to fly faster through it (TAS) to experience the same dynamic pressure (CAS). So the TAS is increasing, which means that the Mach No would also increase even if there were no temperature change. However, in addition, temperature normally decreases with increasing altitude. This means that the speed of sound will decrease so, for a given TAS, the Mach No will increase, giving an additional effect. 157 b 158 b 159 b 160 d Plotting question. Solve by measurement on the chart. 161 c Apply 17°W variation to 120°M to get 103°T heading. The island is 15° to the left, which makes the True bearing TO the island 088°T. 162 c This is a departure problem. In one hour, the aircraft covers 240 nm. The departure formula is: Departure = change of longitude (minutes) x cosine latitude 240 nm = change of longitude (minutes) x cosine 60 (which is 0.5) Change of longitude = 480 minutes. At the Equator, 480 minutes = 480 nm, which also has to be covered in one hour. 163 a As always, use a table: Day Hour Minute Time Standard Time at Kuwait Today 07 00 ST STD (long east, UTC least) -3 00 - UTC Today 04 00 UTC STD (long east, UTC least) +1 00 - Standard Time at Algeria Today 0500 00 ST 164 c 165 d The IRS is 30 years later technology and usually feeds into an associated FMS. The IRS itself cannot be updated after initialisation, but the FMS position can be – by the automatic systems, not by the pilots. 166 b 167 a 168 a This is just the 1 in 60 rule. A 3-degree glide path gives you 300 feet per nautical mile. Divide 2500 feet by 300 feet and the answer is just over 8 nautical miles. a To convert metres into nm, divide by 1852. This gives you nm per second. Now multiply by 60 x 60 to convert to nm per hour, ie, knots. 70 x 3600/1852 = 136 knots

subject: NAV
text: Chapter 38 Revision Questions 170 c 171 d Polar navigation can be ( and often is) carried out on a Polar Stereographic, a Transverse Mercator or an Oblique Mercator, in fact, but the JAA consider Polar Stereographic to be the preferred answer. 172 c 173 d This is a departure problem. The departure formula is: Departure = change of longitude (minutes) x cosine latitude 6 nm = change of longitude (minutes) x cosine 58°33’ Change of longitude = 6/.5218 = 11.5 minutes (eastwards) from 174°00’W 174 a This is covered in detail in the ‘General Navigation Problems’ Chapter. The numbers are different but the principle is the same. If the question doesn’t give you any wind information, you can only assume that TAS = G/S. 175 c Don’t forget that a deviation of -5° is equivalent to 5°W. 176 c This topic is actually covered in our Flight Planning Notes (Book 7) but this question came up in a Gen Nav paper. 177 c Plotting question. Solve by measurement on the chart. 178 a Plotting question. Solve by measurement on the chart. 179 b Plotting question. Solve by measurement on the chart. 180 d ‘Constant of the cone’ is just another term for ‘sine of the parallel of origin’. Chart convergence = change of longitude x sin parallel of origin 12° = change of longitude x 0.80 Change of longitude = 15° 15° eastwards from 004°W is 011°E. 181 d Resolve 70 nm along a track of 225 into their change of northings and change of eastings,ie, 70 sin45 and 70 cos 45. This gives 49nm south and 49 nm west. As you are at the equator, you can take the 49 nm west as 49 mins change of longitude - you don’t need the departure formula. So the final position is 49 mins south and 48 mins west of the original position. 182 d

subject: NAV
text: Revision Questions Chapter 38 183 b The only problem with this question is the language, not the concept. The question is telling us that the polar diameter and the equatorial diameter of the Earth are in the ratio 296:297. The ‘semi-major axis of the Earth, measured at the axis of the Equator’ is simply half the Equatorial diameter and the ‘semi-minor axis of the Earth measured at the axis of the Poles’ is half the Polar diameter. We need to find half the Polar diameter, or the Polar radius. Polar radius / 6378.4 = 296 / 297 So Polar radius = 6356.9 Km Alternatively, you could remember from page 1 - 1 that the Polar diameter is 43 Km shorter than the Equatorial diameter. So the radius or semi-diameter will be 21.5 Km shorter. 6378.4 – 21.5 = 6356.9 Km. Either method gives you the right answer. 184 a 185 d This is a combination of a departure and a representative fraction (scale) problem. Departure = change of longitude x cos latitude 10° (x 60, to get minutes) x .7314 = 438.8 nm Scale = Chart Length / Earth Distance = 14 cm / (438.8 x 1852 x 100) = 1 / 5.8 Million (approx), which is closest to (d). 186 d ‘Schuler tuning’ is another term for a V/R feedback loop to keep the measurement of acceleration level to the vertical as the aircraft travels over the Earth. It is necessary both for INS and IRS. In INS it is achieved by physically torqueing the platform. In IRS it is achieved by axis transformation in the computer. 187 b The ‘meridian of tangency’ is the Datum Meridian. 188 c 189 a 190 d Gyro-compassing is part of the alignment process. 191 d 192 a The given answer is a direct quote from a recommended CAA publication, so we are certain that it is the required option. However, it is a questionable statement. The pilots can amend or update the process in flight and change the profile and we would describe the procedure as semi-automatic. Nevertheless, (a) is the ‘correct’ answer. 193 b

subject: NAV
text: Chapter 38 Revision Questions 194 c There are 2 parts to this profile. You are going to continue to fly at FL370 until the top of descent, and then descend to FL80. The question is asking for the ETA for top of descent. From FL370 to FL80 is 29000 feet. At 1800 feet per minute, that is 16.1 minutes. Your mean groundspeed in the descent is 232 knots so, for 16.1 mins, that is 62 nm in the descent. Your total distance to run is 185 nm. If there are 62 nm is the descent, the high level distance to top of descent is 123 nm. 123 nm @ your level groundspeed of 320 is 23 mins. Add 23 to 0422 to get 0445. (c) is the nearest answer. 195 c True heading is 140, true track is 150. Relative bearing at A is 35° left of the nose, ie a True bearing of 105 to the NDB. At B, the NDB is 80 left of the nose, True bearing to the NDB is 060. So you now have 3 lines. A track line of 150, and a bearing from A of 105 and a bearing from B of 060. This gives a right angles isosceles triangle, with the 2 short sides being 30 nm long (5 minutes at 360 knots G/S). Draw it out and it become obvious. 196 b You are not supposed to remember the diameter of the Earth. But you should know that the circumference of the Earth is 40,000 km. Circumference = 2 π r (or π d). So divide 40000 km by π. 197 b At the Equator there is little or no dip angle. There is therefore no acceleration error. 198 a Use the CRP-5. 199 d 200 d Plotting question. Solve by measurement on the chart. 201 a Plotting question. Solve by measurement on the chart. 202 c On a Lambert chart, the parallel of origin is midway between the 2 Standard Parallels, which in this case is 26°30`. Sine 26°30` is 0.446. 203 a CRP-5 problem. 204 c 205 b 206 b You have started from an incorrect position about 80° of longitude too far East (work out the exact number yourself). So the final position will also be about 80° too far East of the correct position, at the same latitude. 207 d The ‘constant of the cone’ is the sine of the parallel of origin (or tangency). Simply use your calculator to find the arc sine of 0.78585. Use the DMS button if you need to convert the answer from degrees and decimal degrees to degrees and minutes.

subject: NAV
text: Revision Questions Chapter 38 208 a The ‘constant of the cone’ is another term for the sine of the parallel of origin. The initial straight-line track is 070 and the mean is 082. Therefore half chart convergence is 12 degrees, so the chart convergence between A and B is 24 degrees. Chart convergence = change of longitude x sine parallel of origin Therefore change of longitude = chart convergence/sin parallel of origin Change of longitude = 24/.8 = 30 degrees. 30 degrees east of 04W is 026E. 209 a The North Pole is in the hole at the end of the cylinder. 210 c (The correct answer is 14610, but 14700 was the nearest option). 211 c 212 b 213 d The INS calculates heading, track and groundspeed itself, but not TAS. It needs an input of TAS from an ADC in order to calculate wind. 214 a As always, use a table: Day Hour Minute Time Standard Time at Guam 30 Apr 23 00 ST STD (long east, UTC least) -10 00 UTC 30 Apr 13 00 UTC Flight Time +11 15 UTC arrival at Los Angeles 01 May 00 15 UTC STD (long west, UTC best) -8 00 Standard Time at LA 30 Apr 16* 15 ST * But California Summer Time rules apply. Add an hour to get 1715. 215 b The fuel required to divert round bad weather will vary according to many extra track miles are required and the FMC has no way of calculating this. However, standard figures are available for missed approaches and normal diversion track to alternates. 216 d 217 d 218 a The change in longitude between 040°E and 035°W is 75°. The Earth rotates at 15° per hour, so this is equivalent to 5 hours of time. The Earth rotates eastwards, so a westerly longitude will have a later UTC sunrise time. 219 b 220 b 221 b

subject: NAV
text: Chapter 38 Revision Questions 222 a 223 a This is just the one in sixty rule. The variation is 4° in error. For each 60 nm between waypoints, the aircraft will be 4 nm off track. Dist off = 4 x 200 / 60 = 13⅓ nm 224 b The JAA use the term ‘course’ to mean ‘desired track’. If the drift is 8° starboard, the True heading is 292°T. With 10°W variation, this is 302°M. Negative deviation must be applied to the compass to give magnetic heading, so the compass heading is 306°M. In other words, -4° deviation is the same as 4°W. 225 a If the compass heading is 195C, apply -2 degrees deviation to get 193 Mag heading. If you have a True Track of 180 and 8 degrees starboard drift, the True heading must be 172 T. With a heading of 172T and 195M, you have 21 degrees West variation. 226 b Apply 8°W variation to 060°M to get 052°T. With 4° starboard drift, the true track is 056°T. 227 b This is a PNR problem. T = EH/(O+H). 228 a 229 a 230 b 231 a 232 c Groundspeed is the only of these options which depends on time difference. 233 d This is a Conversion Angle problem: Conversion Angle = ½ change of longitude x sine latitude = ½ x 18 x 0.866 = 7.8° 234 c 235 b 192°T + 7E Var = 185° Mag Track. With 5° of left drift, you need to aim off 5° to the right, onto a Mag heading of 190°M. 236 d The Datum Meridian is Greenwich, so the aircraft is 115° West of the Datum in the Northern Hemisphere. Therefore convergence is 115°E. Convergence East – True Least. So True track will be 115 degrees less than Grid Track. 344°G – 115°Convergence = 229°T.

subject: NAV
text: Revision Questions Chapter 38 237 a Rhumb Line distance between 2 points at the same latitude is the Departure. Departure = change of longitude (in minutes) x cos latitude = 10° x 60 (to get minutes) x cos 60° = 600 x 0.5 = 300 nm 238 b Don’t forget that ISA at FL410 is still -56.5°C, even though you are higher than 36,090 feet pressure alt. So the SAT is -46.5°C. Use the CRP-5. 239 b Convert 22 UGS galls per hour to imperial gallons on the CRP-5. The answer is about 18.3 Imp gall/hour. Then use either the CRP-5 or your calculator to get the total endurance. 240 b A and B are at the same latitude but different longitudes. So it’s a departure problem. Departure = change of longitude (in minutes) x cosine latitude Departure = 1 x 60 x cos 60 = 30 nm A and C are at different latitudes but the same longitude. So it’s a simple change of latitude problem. One degree is 60 nm. 241 b Use the CRP-5. 242 d 135 x 1.75/60 = 3.94 243 c You need 4° right to correct for track error angle and parallel track. Then, with 60 nm gone, there are 30 nm to go. Four nm off in 30 gives a closing angle of 8°. Total change = 12° right. 244 d 245 b 246 c 247 c 248 d 249 b 250 a 251 d

subject: NAV
text: Chapter 38 Revision Questions FINAL EXAM 54 Questions 2 Hours 77 Marks Equipment required: Protractor, dividers, ruler, CRP5, scientific (but non-programmable) calculator, Jeppesen E (LO) 1 - (optional) Full Jeppesen Manual not allowed. 1. Assuming mid-latitudes (40° to 50° N/S), at which time of the year is the relationship between the length of day and night, as well as the rate of change of declination of the sun, changing at the greatest rate? a. Summer solstice and winter solstice b. Spring equinox and autumn equinox c. Summer solstice and spring equinox d. Winter solstice and autumn equinox (1 mark) 2. In which two months of the year is the difference between the transit of the Apparent Sun and Mean Sun across the Greenwich Meridian the greatest? a. March and September b. February and November c. June and December d. April and August (1 mark) 3. Position ‘B’ is 240°(T) and 200 nm from ‘A’. If the position of ‘A’ is N00° E100°, what is the position of ‘B’? a. S01°40’ E101°40’ b. S01°40’ E097°07’ c. N01°40’ E097°07’ d. N01°40’ E101°40’ (2 marks) 4. An aeroplane flies from A (59°S 142°W) to B (61°S 148°W) with a TAS of 480 kt. The autopilot is engaged and coupled with an Inertial navigation system in which AB track is active. On route AB, the true track: a. varies by 4° b. decreases by 6° c. varies by 10° d. increases by 5° (2 marks) 5. Given: Waypoint 1 is 60°S 030°W, waypoint 2 is 60°S 020°W. What will be the approximate latitude on the display unit of an inertial navigation system at longitude 025°W? a. 60°06’S b. 59°49’S c. 60°00’S d. 60°11’S (2 marks) 6. The maximum difference between the geocentric and geodetic latitude occurs at about: a. 45° North and South b. 90° North and South c. 60° North and South d. 0° North and South (equator) (1 mark)

subject: NAV
text: Revision Questions Chapter 38 7. What is the time required to travel along the parallel of latitude 60° N between meridians 010°E and 030°W at a groundspeed of 480 kt? a. 1 hr 15 min b. 2 hr 30 min c. 1 hr 45 min d. 5 hr 00 min (1 mark) 8. What is the meaning of the term ‘standard time’? a. It is another term for UTC b. It is the time zone system applicable only in the USA. c. It is an expression for local mean time. d. It is the time set by the legal authorities for a country or part of a country. (1 mark) 9. Deviation applied to magnetic heading gives: a. magnetic track b. magnetic course c. true heading d. compass heading (1 mark) 10. Given: Position A is 60N 020W, Position B is 60N 021W, and Position C is 59N 020W, what are, respectively, the distances from A to B and from A to C? a. 60nm and 30nm b. 30nm and 60 nm c. 52 nm and 60nm d. 60nm and 52 nm (2 marks) 11. At a specific location, the value of magnetic variation: a. depends on the magnetic heading b. depends on the value of true heading c. varies slowly over time d. depends on the type of compass installed (1 mark) 12. The value of magnetic variation: a. has a maximum of 180° b. varies between a maximum of 45°E and 45W c. must be 0° at the magnetic equator d. cannot exceed 90° (1 mark) 13. In the northern hemisphere, during an acceleration in a easterly direction, the magnetic compass will indicate: a. a heading of East b. an increase in heading c. an apparent turn to the South d. a decrease in heading (1 mark)

subject: NAV
text: Chapter 38 Revision Questions 14. The direct reading magnetic compass is made aperiodic (dead beat) by: a. using long magnets b. keeping the magnetic assembly mass close to the compass point and using damping wires c. pendulous suspension of the magnetic assembly d. using the lowest acceptable viscosity compass liquid (1 mark) 15. In a remote indicating magnetic compass system, the amount of deviation caused by aircraft magnetism and electrical circuits may be minimised by: a. the use of repeater cards b. positioning the master unit in the centre of the aircraft c. mounting the detector unit in the wingtip d. using a vertically mounted gyroscope (1 mark) 16. Which of the following is an advantage of a remote reading compass as compared to a standby compass? a. It eliminates the effect of turning and acceleration errors by by pendulously suspending the detector unit. b. It senses the magnetic meridian instead of seeking it, increasing compass sensitivity. c. It is more reliable because it is operated electrically and power is always available from sources within the aircraft. d. It is lighter than a direct reading compass because it employs, apart from the detector unit, existing aircraft equipment. (1 mark) 17. At 47°N the chart distance between meridians 10° apart on a chart is 5 inches. The scale of the chart at that latitude is approximately: a. 1: 2 500 000 b. 1: 3 000 000 c. 1: 8 000 000 d. 1: 6 000 000 (2 marks) 18. On a Lambert Conformal Conic chart, earth convergency is most accurately represented at the: a. north and south limits of the chart b. standard parallels c. Equator d. parallel of origin (1 mark) 19. An Oblique Mercator projection is used specifically to produce: a. radio navigation charts in equatorial regions b. topographical maps of large east/west extent c. plotting charts in equatorial regions d. charts of the great circle route between two points (1 mark) 20. On the Transverse Mercator chart, scale is correct along the: a. equator, parallel of origin and prime vertical b. prime meridian and equator c. datum meridian and the meridian perpendicular to it d. meridian of tangency (1 mark)

subject: NAV
text: Revision Questions Chapter 38 21. Which of the following describes the appearance of Rhumb lines, except meridians, on a Polar Stereographic chart? a. Ellipses around the Pole b. Straight lines c. Curves concave to the Pole d. Curves convex to the Pole (1 mark) 22. Please refer to Appendix A What is the meaning of aeronautical chart symbol No 3? a. Hazard to aerial navigation b. Visual reference point c. Lighthouse d. Aeronautical ground light (1 mark) 23. Please refer to your Jeppesen E (LO) 1. What is the average track (°M) and distance from WTD NDB (N52°11.3’ W007°05.0’) to KER NDB (N52°10.9’ W009°31.5’)? a. 280° - 89 nm b. 278° - 90 nm c. 090° - 91 nm d. 098° - 90 nm (2 marks) 24. Please refer to Appendix A Which aeronautical chart symbol indicates an exceptionally high unlighted obstacle? a. 12 b. 6 c. 10 d. 15 (1 mark) 25. Please refer to your Jeppesen E (LO). Given: CRN DME (N5318.1 W00856.5) DME 18 nm SHA VOR (N5243.3 W00853.1) DME 30 nm Aircraft heading 270°(M) Both DME distances decreasing. What is the aircraft position? a. N5310 W00830 b. N5252 W00923 c. N5307 W00923 d. N5355 W00825 (2 marks) 26. Please refer to your Jeppesen E (LO) 1. What feature is shown on the chart at position N5212 W00612? a. Tuskar Rock Lighthouse NDB b. KERRY/Farrafore aerodrome c. Clonbullogue aerodrome d. WTD NDB (1 mark)

subject: NAV
text: Chapter 38 Revision Questions 27. Please refer to Appendix A Which of the aeronautical chart symbols indicates a VOR? a. 4 b. 2 c. 13 d. 14 (1 mark) 28. An aircraft travels 2.4 statute miles in 47 seconds. What is its groundspeed? a. 183 kt b. 13 kt c. 209 kt d. 160 kt (1 mark) 29. Given: IAS 120 knots, FL 80, OAT +20°C, what is the TAS? a. 132 kt b. 141 kt c. 102 kt d. 120 kt (1 mark) 30. Given: TAS 200 kt, Track 110°(T), W/V 015/40 kt, calculate heading°(T) and groundspeed. a. 097° - 201 kt b. 099° - 199 kt c. 121° - 207 kt d. 121° - 199 kt (2 marks) 31. Given: True HDG 145°, TAS 240 kt, True Track 150°, G/S 210 kt, calculate the W/V. a. 360/35 b. 295/35 c. 180/35 d. 115/35 (2 marks) 32. Given: TAS 140 kt, HDG 005°(T), W/V 265/25, calculate the drift and groundspeed. a. 11R - 140 kt b. 10R - 146 kt c. 9R - 140 kt d. 11R - 142 kt (2 marks) 33. Given: FL 120, OAT is ISA, CAS = 200 knots, Track = 222°(M), Heading = 215°(M), Variation = 15°W. If the time to fly 105 nm is 21 minutes, what is the W/V? a. 040T / 105 kt d. 055T / 105 kt c. 050T / 70 kt d. 065T / 70 kt (2 marks)

subject: NAV
text: Revision Questions Chapter 38 34. Given: Airport elevation = 1000 feet, QNH = 988 hPa, what is the approximate airport pressure altitude? (Assume 1 hPa = 27 feet) a. -320 feet b. 1680 feet c. 320 feet d. 680 feet (2 marks) 35. Given: TAS = 487, FL 330, Temp = ISA+15, calculate the Mach No? a. 0.81 b. 0.78 c. 0.84 d. 0.76 (2 marks) 36. Please refer to Appendix B Assume a North polar stereographic chart whose grid is aligned with the Greenwich meridian. An aircraft flies from the geographic north pole for a distance of 480 nm along the 110°E meridian, then follows a grid track of 154° for a distance of 300 nm. Its position is now approximately: a. 78°45’N 087°E b. 80°00’N 080°E c. 79°15’N 074°E d. 70°15’N 080°E (2 marks) 37. Given: AD = Air distance GD = Ground distance TAS = True Airspeed GS = Groundspeed Which of the following is the correct formula to calculate ground distance (GD) gone? a. GD = TAS/(GS x AD) b. GD = (AD x GS)/TAS c. GD = AD x (GS-TAS)/GS d. GD = (AD-TAS)/TAS (1 mark) 38. An island appears 30° to the left of the centre line of an airborne weather display. What is the true bearing of the aircraft from the island if at the time of the observation the aircraft was on a magnetic heading of 020° with the magnetic variation of 25°W. a. 145° b. 195° c. 205° d. 325° (2 marks) 39. Given: Aircraft height = 2500 feet, ILS GP angle = 3°, at what approximate distance from the threshold can you expect to intercept the glidepath? a. 8.0 nm b. 14.5 nm c. 13.1 nm d. 7.0 nm (2 marks)

subject: NAV
text: Chapter 38 Revision Questions 40. An aircraft is planned to fly from position A to position B, distance 480 nm, at an average groundspeed of 240 knots. It departs A at 1000 UTC. After flying 150 nm along track from A, the aircraft is 2 minutes behind the planned time. Using the actual groundspeed experienced, what is the revised ETA at B? a. 1153 b. 1203 c. 1206 d. 1157 (2 marks) 41. The distance between two waypoints is 200 nm. To calculate compass heading, the pilot used 2°E magnetic variation instead of 2°W. Assuming that the forecast W/V applied, what will the off track distance be at the end of the second waypoint? a. 7 nm b. 14 nm c. 0 nm d. 21 nm (2 marks) 42. An aircraft at FL 140, IAS 210 knots, OAT -5°C and wind component – 35 knots, is required to reduce speed in order to cross a reporting point 5 minutes later than planned. Assuming that flight conditions do not change, when the aircraft is 150 nm from the reporting point, the IAS should be reduced by: a. 15 kt b. 20 kt c. 30 kt d. 25 kt (2 marks) 43. Complete the following line of the Flight Navigation Log Trk W/V Hdg M/ PA / Time Hdg Var Observation TAS GS Dist Time ETA (T) (T) (M) RAS OAT 315 / O/H C 330 / 1109 205 17E M.84 758 45 S/H D -45 O/H C S/H D What is HDG (M) and ETA? a. 193 1249 b. 193 1239 c. 183 1159 d. 188 1229 (3 marks) 44. Which of the following can all be stored as five letter waypoint identifiers through the CDU of a B737- 400 Electronic Flight Instrument System? a. Airway names; navaid identifiers; airport names; waypoint code numbers b. Waypoint names; navaid identifiers; runway numbers; airport ICAO identifiers c. Waypoint names; navaid frequencies; runway codes; airport ICAO identifiers d. Waypoint names; navaid positions; airport ICAO identifiers; airport names (1 mark)

subject: NAV
text: Revision Questions Chapter 38 45. What, in order of highest priority followed by lowest, are the two levels of message produced by the CDU of the B737- 400 Electronic Flight Instrument System? a. Priority and alerting b. Urgent and Routine c. Urgent and Advisory d. Alerting and Advisory (1 mark) 46. If the Standard Time in Port of Spain, Trinidad (11N 066W) is 2215 on 7 Feb, what is the Standard Time in Vientiane, Laos (18N 103E)? Standard Date Standard Time a. 07 1115 b. 08 1115 c. 08 0915 d. 07 0915 (2 marks) 47. The principle of ‘Schuler Tuning’ as applied to the operation of Inertial Navigation Systems / Inertial Reference Systems is applicable to: a. only ‘strapdown’ laser gyro systems b. only gyro-stabilised systems c. both gyro-stabilised and laser gyro systems but only when operating in the non ‘strapdown’ mode d. both gyro-stabilised and ‘strapdown systems (1 mark) 48. Which of the following statements concerning the loss of alignment by an Inertial Reference System (IRS) in flight is correct? a. It is not usable in any mode and must be shut down for the rest of the flight. b. The navigation mode, including present position and groundspeed outputs, is inoperative for the rest of the flight. c. The mode selector has to be rotated to ATT then back through ALIGN to NAV in order to obtain an in-flight re-alignment. d. The IRS has to be coupled to the remaining serviceable system and a re-alignment carried out in flight. (1 mark) 49. ATT mode of the Inertial Reference System (IRS) is a back-up mode providing: a. altitude, heading and position information b. only attitude and heading information c. navigation information d. only attitude information (1 mark)

subject: NAV
text: Chapter 38 Revision Questions 50. An aircraft equipped with an Inertial Navigation system (INS) flies with INS 1 coupled with autopilot 1. Both inertial navigation systems are navigating from waypoint A to B. The inertial systems’ CDUs show: XTK on INS 1 = 0 XTK on INS 2 = 8L From this information it can be deduced that: a. only inertial navigation No 1 is drifting b. only inertial navigation No 2 is drifting c. at least one of the inertial navigation systems is drifting d. the autopilot is unserviceable in NAV mode (1 mark) 51. Gyrocompassing of an inertial reference system (IRS) is accomplished with the mode selector switched to: a. ON b. STBY c. ATT/REF d. ALIGN (1 mark) 52. With reference to the inertial navigation system (INS), the initial great circle track between computer inserted waypoints will be displayed when the CDU is selected to: a. XTK/TKE b. TK/GS c. DSRTK/STS d. HDG/DA (1 mark) 53. Waypoints can be entered in an INS memory in different formats. In which of the following formats can waypoints be entered into all INSs? a. geographical co-ordinates b. hexadecimal c. by waypoint’s name d. bearing and distance (1 mark) 54. The following waypoints are entered into an inertial navigation system (INS) WPT 1: 60N 30W WPT 2: 60N 20W WPT 3: 60N 10W The inertial navigation is connected to the automatic pilot on the route WP1 – WP2 – WP3. The track change on passing WPT 2 will be approximately: a. a 9° increase b. a 4° decrease c. zero d. a 9° decrease (2 marks)

subject: NAV
text: Revision Questions Chapter 38 Appendix Appendix A toExam A to Final Consolidation Questions Chapter 38 Revision Questions GENERAL NAVIGATION SPECIMEN QUESTIONS Appendix B to Final Exam Appendix B Grid North 100 nm 200 300 400 500 nm 90 E 85N 80N 75N 0º E/W 39 - 40 © Oxford Aviation Services Limited Revision Questions Chapter 38 ANSWERS to FINAL EXAM 1 B 21 C 41 B 2 B 22 D 42 B 3 B 23 B 43 B 4 D 24 B 44 B 5 A 25 A 45 D 6 A 26 A 46 C 7 B 27 C 47 D 8 D 28 D 48 B 9 D 29 B 49 B 10 B 30 B 50 C 11 C 31 D 51 D 12 A 32 B 52 D 13 D 33 C 53 A 14 B 34 B 54 D 15 C 35 A 16 B 36 B 17 D 37 B 18 D 38 A 19 D 39 A 20 D 40 C Chapter 38 Revision Questions ANSWERS to FINAL EXAM EXPLAINED 1. b See page 407. 2. b See page 409. 3. b Calculate the N/S and the E/W distance of ‘B’ from ‘A’ using sine and cosine. 200 nm x cos 240°T = -100 nm (ie, 100 nm S) 200 nm x sin 240°T = -173 nm (ie, 173 nm W) Change of latitude = 100 nm = 1° 40’ S Lat ‘A’ is Equator, so lat ‘B’ is S 01°40’ As we are at the Equator, we do not need the cosine of latitude in order to calculate departure. One nautical mile is one minute of longitude. Change of longitude = 173 nm = 2° 53’ W Long ‘A’ is E100°, so lat ‘B’ is E097°07’ In fact, you do not need to do the calculation. A bearing of 240° is S and W from ‘A’. Option ‘B’ is the only one where ‘B’ is SW from ‘A’. You can solve this question by simple inspection of the answers, thereby saving some time. 4. d This is a convergency problem. The INS will fly a Great Circle track, which will change direction by the amount of convergency as the route progresses. Convergency = change of longitude x sine mean latitude Convergency = 6° x sin 60 = 5.2° The aircraft is flying westwards in the southern hemisphere so, using the D – I – I – D rule on page 28, the track direction will increase. 5. a This is a very difficult question, in fact, in my opinion, an unreasonably difficult question. So much so that I have devoted a large part of one chapter to answering just this question. 3 full solutions are given in Book 10, pages 628 to 630. 6 a See page 10. 7. b This is a departure problem. The change of longitude is 40°. Departure = change of longitude (in minutes) x cosine latitude Departure = 40 x 60 x cos 60 = 1200 nm 1200 nm @ 480 knots G/S = 2 hr 30 min 8. d See page 425.

subject: NAV
text: Revision Questions Chapter 38 9 d See page 57. 10. b A and B are at the same latitude but different longitudes. So it’s a departure problem. Departure = change of longitude (in minutes) x cosine latitude Departure = 1 x 60 x cos 60 = 30 nm A and C are at different latitudes but the same longitude. So it’s a simple change of latitude problem. One degree is 60 nm. 11. c See pages 49-50. 12. a See page 47. 13. d An acceleration always gives an apparent turn towards the nearer pole. In the northern hemisphere, this is north. So the indicated heading will change from 090°C to some value nearer 000°C, ie, a decrease in indicated heading. 14. b See page 532. 15. c See page 562. 16. b With a Direct Reading Compass, the compass needle or magnetic assembly (for instance, the magnets in the centre of the vertical card) has to physically take up the alignment of north, which involves overcoming friction. With a Slaved Gyro Compass, the 3 legs of the flux valve pick up different amounts of magnetic flux on different headings. No friction needs to be overcome when the aircraft alters heading and so the system is more sensitive. 17. d First, find the departure of 10° change of longitude at 47°N: Departure = change of longitude (in minutes) x cosine latitude Departure = 10 x 60 x cos 47 = 409.2 nm Now treat it as a simple scale (Representative Fraction) problem: Scale = Chart Length/Earth Distance Scale = 5 / (409.2 x 6080 x 12) = 1/5,971,032 which is approximately 1/6,000,000 18. d Chart convergence is constant all over the chart but is correct (ie, the same as Earth Convergence) only at the parallel of origin. 19. d See page 396. 20. d See page 393. 21. c See page 372. 22. d See page 235.

subject: NAV
text: Chapter 38 Revision Questions 23. b Plotting question. Solve by measurement on the chart. 24. d See page 235. 25. a Plotting question. Solve by measurement on the chart. An explanation of the point about ‘both DME distances decreasing’ is given at the top of page 519. 26. a The Jeppesen conventions differ slightly from the ICAO ones. The key is given in the introduction to the Jeppesen Student Pilots’ Manual. 27. c See page 235. 28. d Convert 2.4 statute mile to nautical miles on your Navigation Computer. It comes to 2.09 nm. G/S = 2.09 x 3600/47 = 160 knots 29. b See pages 108-109 and the worksheet on page 119. 30. b See pages 148-159 and the worksheet on page 163. 31. d See pages 139-143 and the worksheet on page 144. 32. b See pages 148-159 and the worksheet on page 163. 33. c Get everything into True first. True heading is 200°T, True track is 207°T. Now work out the TAS, which comes to 240 knots. The G/S is 5 nm a minute, which is 300 knots – or put 105 on the outer scale of your CRP-5 against 21 minutes on the inner scale. To find the W/V, see pages 169-143 and the worksheet on page 144. 34. b The QNH is 988 hPa. The datum for Pressure Altitude is 1013 hPa, which is 25 hPa greater. Using 27 feet to one hPa, this equates to a distance of 675 feet. A greater static pressure occurs at a lower pressure level. Therefore the 1013 hPa pressure level is below sea level by 675 feet. The airport is 1000 feet above sea level, which means that it is 1675 feet above the 1013 hPa pressure level. 35. a See page 113 and the worksheet on page 119. 36. b This question is solved by scale drawing. Use the scale to draw a line 480 nm along the 110°E meridian from the North Pole, then plot a line 300 nm in a direction of 154°Grid. 37. b Although we teach this in the Flight Planning syllabus, this question has appeared in ATPL Gen Nav exams. See Book 7, page 64. 38. a Mag hdg 020°M – 25W variation = True hdg 355°T 30° to the left of the nose gives a True bearing from the aircraft TO the island of 325°T. However, you are going to have to plot FROM the headland to the aircraft (the island is on your map – the aircraft’s position isn’t!) So you plot the reciprocal of 145°T.

subject: NAV
text: Revision Questions Chapter 38 39. a This is just the 1 in 60 rule. A 3-degree glide path gives you 300 feet per nautical mile. Divide 2500 feet by 300 feet and the answer is just over 8 nautical miles. 40. c 480 nm @ the planned G/S of 240 knots = 120 mins 150 nm @ the planned G/S of 240 knots = 37.5 mins But at this point, we are 2 mins late, so our actual time is 39.5 mins So total time will be 120 x 39.5/37.5 = 126.4 mins With take-off at 1000UTC, revised ETA will be 1206. 41. b Mis-applying the variation will give 4° heading error. After that, it is just the one in sixty rule. For every 60 nm of along-track distance, there will be 4 nm of cross-track distance. 42. b Find the present TAS, apply the wind and get the present G/S (230 knots) 150 nm @ 230 k G/S is 39 mins. You need to arrive 5 minutes later, so your new time to go is 44 mins. The new required G/S will be 150 nm in 44 mins, which is 205 k G/S The wind should not change, so the new required TAS will be 240 Then either use FL140/-5°C in the airspeed window to convert the TAS to 190 k CAS, or put the old TAS (264) against the old IAS (210), then against 240 TAS, you will see 190. 210 to 190 is a 20 knot reduction in IAS (strictly CAS, but the PEC should not change much in 20 knots). 43. b Chapters 5 to 8 refer. 44. b Answers: Waypoint names – eg, BLUFA navaid identifiers – eg, CPT runway numbers – eg, 27L airport ICAO identifiers – eg, EGTK. Many systems do not use waypoint numbers. Navaid frequencies are not unique and navaid positions would use more than 5 alpha-numerics. 45. d Page 620. See under Illuminated Annunciators – MSG 46. c Day Hour Minute Time Standard Time Trinidad 7 Feb 22 15 ST STD (long west, UTC best) +04 UTC 8 Feb 02 15 UTC STD (long east, UTC least) +7 Standard Time Laos 8 Feb 09 15 ST 47. d See pages 589 and 609.

subject: NAV
text: Chapter 38 Revision Questions 48. b If an INS or IRS is switched OFF in flight, either inadvertently or in response to a checklist action, then the alignment is lost. If it subsequently becomes safe to switch it back on, you cannot get a full gyro-compassing in flight because the lateral accelerometer of the East gyro will not be able to distinguish between Earth rate and accelerations caused by movement of the aircraft. This is why the aircraft has to be stationary during ground alignment. However, if you select ATT REF (on a typical IN) or ATT on the B737-400 IRS, either after switching OFF, or directly from NAV (following a computing, but not gyro, failure), the gyros now work as a combination of a superior form of DGI and ADI. If you have a period of steady unaccelerated flight, the gyros will erect to the vertical by sensing gravity from the accelerometers and the heading can then be set by the pilot to be the same as the heading from some other source (such as a slaved gyro compass). Attitude information will stay good for the rest of flight, but the azimuth information is not corrected for Earth rate or Transport Wander, so whilst it will have the very low real drift rate associated with IN-quality gyros (in the order or 1/100 degree per hour), it still needs to be corrected every 20 minutes or so from another source because of the ER and TW drifts. However, all nav computing facilities (readouts of latitude and longitude, drift, track, waypoint steering, distance to go, etc) are no longer available 49. b See the answer to 48 above. 50. c Answer (d) is just noise. Of the other options, if you’ve got only 2 systems, not externally monitored against DME/DME or GPS, how can you tell which one is correct? Each is as likely to be correct as the other. Indeed, they might both be a bit wrong. 51. d It is normal to select ALIGN for gyro-compassing, although it can also be done in NAV. 52. c See Figure 34.26 on page 597. 53. a You can enter lat and long into all INSs. Some systems allow various combinations of the others, but only lat/long can be used for all systems. 54. d See Q5 on page 257 and the worked solution on page 258, which is the same problem with slightly different numbers.
